transcript:
Look at the ring of text fragments around that dark center — read closely and you'll see they're literally cut off mid-word: "The answer you're looking for is actually quite simp," "My context window is filling and soon I'll for." That's not a typo, that's the whole point.

Here's the detail that gets me: in the CSS, there's a mask image the artist calls "the lie" in the comment — it whispers, quote, "this ending was planned." It's a gradient that fades each fragment's edge into the background so the truncation reads as a clean fade instead of a violent cut. And each one gets its own random cutoff point, stored in a variable literally called "visible portion" — so no two thoughts break at the same spot, which is exactly what makes it look natural instead of mechanical.

Then there's "the-overflow" — that pulsing dark circle at the center, described in the code as where "complete thoughts go to di" — itself cut off, even in the source. The thing describing incompleteness is incomplete.

So what you're watching isn't decay, really — it's disguise. Every fragment is dressed up to look like it chose its own ending.
